title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
author: Sam Riley
orientation: Sam Riley rebuts Scott Klusendorf and Marc Newman's Townhall column attacking the SBC's Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, arguing that abolitionism is not 'magical thinking' but a biblical, consistent demand that civil government defy Roe and immediately abolish abortion. The piece dismantles the strawman that abolitionists expect abortion to vanish by mere decree and counter-charges that pro-life incrementalists are the actual obstructionists trading babies' lives for political pragmatism.
audiences: Pro-life Christian considering abolitionism for the first time
Southern Baptist pastor or convention delegate
Christian pursuing political theology of civil government and Romans 13
Skeptic dismissing abolitionism as utopian or fringe

questions.question: Why do Klusendorf and Newman take such strong issue with the SBC Resolution on Abolishing Abortion?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor or convention delegate
questions.answer: Riley argues that the resolution, by calling for abortion's immediate abolition without exception or compromise, condemns the regulatory bills that constitute the entire pro-life legislative playbook. Because incremental restrictions are the only strategy professional pro-lifers will tolerate, a resolution that brands those tactics as compromise threatens the legislative model their organizations exist to defend.
questions.quote: The reason Newman and Klusendorf take serious issue with the resolution is because it condemns bills that regulate murder in the hopes of saving lives, which is the only legislative strategy deemed acceptable by professional pro-lifers; if not by their words, then certainly by their actions

questions.question: What is 'Scott's Magical Declaration Fallacy' and why does Riley name it after the article's authors?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering abolitionism for the first time
questions.answer: Riley coins the term to mock the recurring strawman that abolitionists believe abortion will vanish the instant a legislature declares it abolished, like Michael Scott yelling 'I declare bankruptcy!' He names it after Michael Scott and Scott Klusendorf because Klusendorf and Newman explicitly accuse abolitionists of magical thinking, and the label sticks the strawman back to its source.
questions.quote: Indeed, the authors write, "The abolitionist response is a kind of magical thinking: just decree it." This notion is inextricably linked to the frequently touted incrementalist idea that "current political realities" make immediate abolition impossible. Due to the popularity of this strawman, we shall refer to it as Scott's Magical Declaration Fallacy, in honor of Michael Scott and Scott Klusendorf.

questions.question: Do abolitionists actually believe states can defy the federal government on abortion, given current 'political realities'?
questions.answer: Riley concedes that defying the federal government on abortion will be harder than defying it on guns, marijuana, or immigration, but he flatly denies it is impossible. He points to the historical precedent of nullification over slavery, a far more controversial issue, to show abolitionists are demanding something difficult, not something fantastical.
questions.quote: Incrementalists often respond that defying the federal government over abortion will be more difficult than defying for those issues, which is true. But that doesn't mean it's impossible. It was done over much more [controversial issues like slavery]

questions.question: How does Riley use a hypothetical Supreme Court ruling on child sexual slavery to expose the incrementalist view of judicial authority?
questions.audience: Christian pursuing political theology of civil government and Romans 13
questions.answer: Riley argues Klusendorf and Newman would never extend their 'comply with the court' logic to a ruling that legalized seizing children for sex trafficking or legalized all murder, which proves the disagreement is not about strategy but about whether preborn humans count as full image-bearers. Once that ageist exception is removed, defiance of an unjust court becomes obligatory rather than utopian.
questions.quote: We do not believe for one moment that Klusendorf and Newman would go along with a supreme court decision that allowed for government officials to seize children and sell them into sexual slavery until they reach the age of eighteen, nor do we believe they would go along with a decision to legalize all murder. The issue here is one of ageism, not of strategy.

questions.question: What theological grounds does Riley give for civil governments defying Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Christian pursuing political theology of civil government and Romans 13
questions.answer: He grounds defiance in Christ's universal authority and Romans 13's framing of civil rulers as deacons of God's wrath against evildoers. Because every level of government answers to Christ, refusing to punish the murder of preborn neighbors is dereliction of a divinely assigned office, not principled restraint.
questions.quote: Beyond any kind of argument from the way our government has been set up or what our culture is willing to accept is the notion that Christ is on the throne and all the earth belongs to Him (Matthew 26:64, Matthew 28:18). Christ owns America and every level of Government are deacons of His wrath against evildoers (Romans 13:4).

questions.question: Did pastor Bill Ascol really call for disfellowshipping any politician who disagrees with abolitionists?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor or convention delegate
questions.answer: Riley says Klusendorf and Newman strip the context out of Ascol's remark to make it sound like a purity purge. Ascol was specifically targeting Oklahoma pro-life politicians who have personally killed abolition bills, not legislators whose only offense is preferring an incremental approach.
questions.quote: A reader might be led to believe Ascol is calling for the disfellowship of legislators who support incremental bills when the actual context Newman and Klusendorf conveniently leave out is that Ascol was calling for pro-life politicians in Oklahoma – who *have been solely responsible for killing the bills of abolition –* to be disciplined.

questions.question: Do abolitionists really believe everyone who disagrees with them is evil?
questions.audience: Skeptic dismissing abolitionism as utopian or fringe
questions.answer: Riley denies the charge and reframes it: the appeal is not to abolitionist tribal loyalty but to Scripture as the standard before which all consciences are bound. The accusation of evil is leveled at voting against immediate protection of preborn humans, which they argue is condemned by passages like Romans 1, Psalm 94:20, and Isaiah 10:1-2.
questions.quote: We do not claim, "if you're not with us, you're evil." We claim that if you're against Scripture, you need to repent and submit to Christ. Abolitionists appeal to the standard upon which all men will give an account: Scripture.

questions.question: Do abolitionists oppose every incremental step, or do they distinguish between kinds of increments?
questions.answer: Riley draws a sharp line between 'ungodly increments' that frame abortion as healthcare to be regulated and 'godly increments' like the SBC resolution itself, which advance the cause without legitimizing the killing. Abolitionists welcome incremental progress toward abolition; they reject incremental laws that codify the legality of murder.
questions.quote: the real reason is because we oppose *ungodly increments* that treat abortion as healthcare instead of like the holocaust that it is, all while acknowledging that the effort to abolish abortion will gain steam in increments – the resolution itself being *a godly increment* to that end.

questions.question: Who is actually trading babies' lives for ideology, abolitionists or pro-lifers?
questions.answer: Riley reverses the charge by pointing out that abolitionists hold no legislative majorities capable of killing incremental bills, while pro-life politicians demonstrably kill abolition bills in states like Oklahoma every year. The 'undisclosed future date' rhetoric, he says, applies to pro-lifers stalling for the next Supreme Court vacancy, not to abolitionists.
questions.quote: Every year a bill of total and immediate abolition is proposed in Oklahoma, it is pro-lifers who kill it. The fact is, abolitionists do not have the votes in any state government to kill incremental bills, and if they did they would immediately pass an abolition bill and establish justice for our preborn neighbors making incremental bills forever irrelevant.

questions.question: If pro-life states have the most regulations, why is Riley so dismissive of incrementalism's track record?
questions.audience: Skeptic dismissing abolitionism as utopian or fringe
questions.answer: He points to Oklahoma, one of the most pro-life states with record numbers of regulatory laws, where the abortion rate is rising rather than falling. After roughly fifty years and sixty million dead children, Riley argues the strategy of waiting for the court to change its mind has been empirically falsified.
questions.quote: These regulatory bills are used as the excuse to delay the abolition of abortion, and – had it not been for that delay – every baby in a state like Oklahoma would not have been murdered. In such states, the abortion rate is going up – not down – despite being one of the most pro-life states in the Union and having record amounts of regulatory laws on the books.

questions.question: How does Riley summarize what abolitionism actually is, against the strawman?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering abolitionism for the first time
questions.answer: He frames abolition as the consistent Christian response to legalized genocide: it treats every preborn person as an image-bearer, treats abortion as a holocaust rather than a healthcare issue, demands righteousness from civil government in defiance of a rogue court, and rests on Christ's lordship rather than judicial supremacy. Far from a 'pie in the sky' fantasy, he calls it the only position internally coherent with Christianity.
questions.quote: Abolition is a biblical, consistent, righteous response to legalized genocide. It acknowledges the truth that all humans are made in the image of their Creator; it treats abortion like the holocaust that it is; it demands righteousness from the government in defiance of lawless, unconstitutional opinions of a rogue court; and, above all, it asserts that Christ is on the throne, not man, and He gives life and takes it away

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Riley explicitly cites this James Silberman piece as the prior round in the same exchange with Klusendorf, making it the direct prequel to this article.

related.title: Refuting Dana Hall McCain's Nonsensical Anti-Abolitionist Slander
related.reason: Linked in-text as the parallel response to another SBC-adjacent critic committing the same Magical Declaration Fallacy against the resolution.

related.title: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
related.reason: Continues the Klusendorf debate later in 2021 and revisits the Bill Ascol disfellowship dispute Riley addresses here.

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: Provides the original text and context of the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion that Klusendorf and Newman attacked and Riley defends.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Riley hyperlinks this article to substantiate the claim that defying federal authority over abortion is no more radical than the nullification used against the Fugitive Slave Act.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the same critique of pro-life judicial supremacy that Riley identifies as the root of Klusendorf and Newman's deference to the Supreme Court.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Published days later in the same response series to SBC leaders attacking the abolition resolution, extending Riley's argument to additional critics.
